dialogue.utterance: Unit 75 respond to a GSW at the [CITY] Inn, Room 12.
Hi, I'm Alex, a paramedic with Metro EMS. Can you tell me what happened?
He was shot in the neck, then collapsed behind the door. He's not moving.
He's unresponsive. Eye opening is absent, no verbal response, and no motor response noted.
Airway appears partially blocked with blood in the oropharynx. Inserting an airway now to secure it.
No spontaneous breathing. Providing assisted ventilations with BVM.
No palpable pulse. CPR should begin immediately.
Respiration is zero-no chest rise observed with assisted ventilation ongoing.
No pulse-no peripheral pulse is detectable.
Blood pressure is unobtainable-patient is in cardiac arrest.
No glucose obtained-circulation is absent.
No SpO2 reading-circulation is compromised.
EKG shows ventricular fibrillation-no organized rhythm is present.
Initiating BVM assisted ventilations. Airway is now secured with an oropharyngeal airway.
OPA placed. Ventilations are easy, no resistance noted.
Beginning CPR. Confirming proper compression depth and rate.
Five seconds of compressions now. Airway is not dislodged.
Administering high flow oxygen at 15 LPM via BVM.
No pulse found after 30 seconds of CPR. Continue compressions.
BVM ventilations remain effective. No obstruction is noted.
No measurable BP post-CPR. Continue CPR and fluids via IO.
Preparing for IO access on the left humerus. Need rapid vascular access.
IO needle placed successfully. Establishing IV fluids now.
Giving 250 mL normal saline through the intraosseous line.
Resuming CPR. Check again for the return of pulse in a few minutes.
Administering epinephrine 1:10,000-dose recorded at 13:01.
No sign of pulse resumes. Continue CPR. Rhythm needs re-check.
No measurable blood pressure-patient is still arrested.
Lying in V-Fib again. Preparing for defibrillation.
Defibrillator charged to 200 J. Everyone clear for shock delivery.
Clear! Shock delivered. Resuming compressions immediately now.
Continuing compression. Check airway for blockage or dislodgement.
No change in ventricular rhythm. Another shock may be necessary.
Entry wound noted on the left side of the anterior neck. Exit wound not visible due to blood.
Patient is 30 years old. No recordable medical history or allergies noted.
He has no known medications. Found him after a conflict-don't know his background.
AVPU remains unresponsive. No improvement in consciousness noted.
Initiating general-cardiac arrest (Protocol 2-7). Hot load not utilized.
Transport to [COMPANY_NAME] for trauma team to manage GSW.
Patient secured on reeves stretcher. All straps are in place.
CPR in progress. Transporting under emergency protocol.
Rhythm still V-Fib after shock. No response to current interventions yet.
Hospital was alerted-trauma team is aware of incoming GSW patient.
Patient passed over to ED on reeves stretcher. [CITY] bed ready.
Airway secured and last epinephrine dose given at 13:01-all interventions tracked.
IO line in left humerus-no bleed or dislodgement has occurred during transport.
No spontaneous circulation noted. Full trauma protocol initiated upon arrival.
Rhythm unchanged. No signs of perfusion during transport phase.
OPA not dislodged-ventilations stable throughout the trip.
No event changes recorded-patient remains in arrest state.
Full patient documentation complete prior to arrival at ED.
No interruptions occur. All actions confirmed before handoff.
No verbal response or movement noted during transfer phase.
Trauma team is ready-no complications to transfer process.
Bystander not in contact. Patient never responded to EMTs during care.
EVAC complete-patient delivered under emergency care conditions.
SpO2 and pulse remain zero-it is a full cardiac arrest state.
No additional steps actively taken during transfer-resus continues en route.
Airway and support systems intact-fully managed during the travel.
No extra devices utilized aside from BVM and defibrillator post-CPR.
No indoor staging needed-left directly for the ED into trauma resus.
All meds and fluids given prior to arrival-no delays in care.
No code progress observed-vitals remain absent during travel.
Complete record shared-no missing events or missed interventions.
No ongoing treatment started during transport-interventions were pre-transfer.
ED fully informed of patient status and care given in field.
Full trauma alert is activated-no code delays were encountered.
No field transport changes-followed protocol accurately.
No changes to EMT treatment plan-no ED input needed transport.
Reaffirming GSW neck, V-Fib, CPR, and meds-all per documentation complete.
No media or bystander access-patient care was focused and continuous.
No incident interruption-no missed steps after field treatment.
No care gaps. All field interventions active for ED team to see.
Transport stable. No traffic delay recorded during code.
No internal documentation system errors-complete transfer achieved.
No transfer spillage-patient transition clean to ED staff.
No active signs of life-no indication of recovery seen.
No airway worsening or changing-OPA intact during all steps.
No en route request for more interventions-patient already in full protocol.
No emergency equipment withdrawn-only field tools used during response.
No ED call delay-straight dispatch to emergency hospital intake.
No entry wound worsening due to movement-patient was found unresponsive.
Full care documented in real time-no embellishment involved in EMT records.
No external concern during travel-case handled according to protocol.
No edrug dose deviation-given according to correct field sequence.
No environmental issues noted-motel room secure and free from interference.
No repeat requests from ED end-efficient handoff done under resuscitation.
No incomplete documentation noted-field-ED transition stable.
No stray actions-only those related to GSW recorded during time.
No ePCR formatting issues-complete, accurate operative report.
No Bluetooth dropped-it was used to monitor EKG throughout.
No disoriented note-case is straight and clear in EMS narrative.
No field delay-arrived straight for provider response without interference.
No drug expiration concern-meds used within code window.
No community support involved-only first responders at scene.
No hospital navigation issue-trauma team fully aware of reeves transport.
